summary: Reclassified many object entries in configure.py from Object(Unknown, ...) to Object(NonMatching, ...) for files described by the PR title as missing source files. The change was configuration/progress-metadata only: one file changed, with 337 Unknown-to-NonMatching replacements and no C/source logic changes shown.

key_files.role: Central object list/status configuration; many Object status arguments were changed from Unknown to NonMatching.

what_changed: Changed 337 lines in configure.py, with matching 337 deletions, indicating status-marker replacements rather than additions of new source logic.
Replaced Object(Unknown, "...") with Object(NonMatching, "...") across many MeleeLib, RuntimeLib, and DolphinLib object lists.

Examples from the diff include melee/lb/lb_00CE.c, melee/lb/lbshadow.c, melee/cm/camera.c, melee/pl/plstale.c, melee/mp/mplib.c, melee/ef/eflib.c, melee/ft/ftanim.c, melee/gr/grdisplay.c, melee/mn/mnitemsw.c, melee/it/items entries, melee/if/ifall.c, Runtime/runtime.c, MSL/ansi_fp.c, MetroTRK/mainloop.c, dolphin/ax/AXAlloc.c, and sysdolphin/baselib/quatlib.c.
Some Object(Unknown, ...) entries were intentionally left unchanged in the same lists, so this was a selective audit rather than a blanket Unknown-to-NonMatching conversion.
No PR body, issue comments, reviews, or review comments were present in the provided slice.
smart_moves: Kept the change scoped to configure.py, avoiding incidental source/header churn.
Used the existing Object status enum vocabulary instead of introducing new state names or comments.
Performed a broad but mechanical status cleanup across libraries, making future progress reports and build behavior more accurately reflect object state.
Preserved library grouping and object ordering while changing only the status argument, which minimizes review risk for large configuration edits.
decomp_lessons: Do not let Object(Unknown, ...) become a catch-all when a more precise project status such as NonMatching applies.
When auditing object status metadata, keep replacements mechanical and ordering-preserving so the diff remains reviewable.
A large status-only configure.py change can be low risk if it avoids touching decompiled code and changes only the first Object argument.
Selective status conversion matters: unchanged Unknown entries in the same sections imply contributors should verify each object rather than globally replacing every Unknown.
naming_conventions: Status names are capitalized enum-like identifiers: Matching, NonMatching, Unknown.
configure.py object entries follow Object(Status, "path/to/file.c") inside RuntimeLib, MeleeLib, or DolphinLib groupings.
Existing path casing and hex-style file suffixes were preserved, e.g. lb_00CE.c, lb_00F9.c, ft_081B.c, ft_0C88.c, hsd_40FF.c.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: This PR did not include assembly or C matching tactics; it only changed object status metadata.
The matching-related tactic to reuse is to distinguish NonMatching from Unknown at the object-list level so build/progress tooling has more accurate inputs.
For future mass status edits, change only Object status arguments and leave object ordering, comments, and library grouping intact unless separately justified.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR slice.
searchable_terms: Mark missing source files as NonMatching
follow_up_queries: Which configure.py object states should be Unknown versus NonMatching in current project conventions?
Are there remaining Object(Unknown, ...) entries whose source files now exist and should be reclassified?
Does progress reporting count NonMatching objects differently from Unknown objects after PR 1147?
When adding a new non-matching source file, should contributors mark it NonMatching immediately in configure.py?
